title: Kundalini and Art Therapy: Drawing What You Can't Say

body:
Kundalini and Art Therapy: Drawing What You Can't Say

You're here, aren't you? Probably 3 am, staring at the ceiling, heart doing weird things, mind racing with thoughts you can't even put into words. You've felt it - that electricity, that fire, that something that ripped through your life and left you feeling like a raw nerve ending. Yeah, I know that feeling. I lived it. You're not alone, even if it feels like you're the only one on the planet going through this goddamn thing called kundalini.

When you're in the thick of it, especially when it's gone sideways - what they call a "kundalini crisis" - your brain just can't keep up. It's like your internal hard drive got wiped and then overloaded with data from another dimension. You're seeing things, feeling things, hearing things. Your body is doing its own weird dance. And trying to explain it to anyone? Forget about it. They'll look at you like you've lost your marbles, or worse, try to medicate you into oblivion. So you shut down, you withdraw, and that isolation just makes it all worse.

Here's the thing: you've got to find a way to get this stuff out. It's swirling inside you, a chaotic mess of energy and sensation and terror. Keeping it bottled up? That's a recipe for disaster. It'll just fester, build pressure, and believe me, you don't want that. You need an outlet, something that bypasses the limitations of language, because what you're experiencing often defies words. That's where art comes in. Not "art" like you need to be Picasso. I'm talking about getting messy, making marks, drawing what you can't say.

Why Words Fail When Kundalini Rises

Look, our language is built for consensus reality, right? It's for describing things we can all agree on: "the sky is blue," "that's a chair." But when kundalini starts moving, it cracks open your perception of reality. You're suddenly experiencing non-ordinary states, energy flows, visions, sensations that have no direct translation into everyday vocabulary. How do you describe feeling like lightning is coursing through your veins? Or seeing colors that don't exist in the visible spectrum? Or the terror of your own body shaking uncontrollably for hours?

You can't. Not really. And when you try, you sound crazy. So you pull back. You might even start to believe you *are* crazy. But you're not. You're just experiencing something your mind isn't equipped to process with its usual tools. Your logical, linear brain is trying to make sense of something that's anything but logical or linear. It's like trying to catch smoke with a net. It just slips through.

That's why traditional talk therapy, while helpful for many things, can sometimes fall short when you're in the throes of a full-blown kundalini awakening or crisis. You spend an hour trying to articulate the inarticulable, and you leave feeling more frustrated and misunderstood than when you started. You need a different language, a different way to communicate with yourself and process what's happening. You need to speak the language of the unconscious, the language of symbols and sensations.

Art as a Release Valve

Think of art as a release valve for all that pressure building up inside you. When you're drawing, painting, sculpting - whatever it is - you're not trying to be "good." You're not trying to create a masterpiece. You're just letting it out. It's a direct channel from your inner experience to the outside world, bypassing the censor of your conscious mind. It's raw, it's unfiltered, and it's incredibly powerful.

I remember nights where I'd just sit with a cheap sketchbook and a box of crayons, just scribbling. Not drawing anything specific, just letting my hand move. Sometimes it was dark, jagged lines, reflecting the fear and anger. Sometimes it was swirling colors, representing the strange energy I felt. It didn't make sense to anyone else, but it made sense to me. It was a way to externalize the internal chaos, to give it form, even if that form was abstract and messy.

This isn't about making pretty pictures. It's about expression. It's about giving voice to the voiceless parts of you. It's about moving energy. When you're in a kundalini crisis, there's a hell of a lot of energy moving through you, and if it doesn't have an outlet, it can feel like it's going to tear you apart. Art is one of the safest, most accessible ways to let that energy flow out of you, onto the page or into the clay, instead of staying trapped inside, causing more distress.

Techniques for When You're Too Scared to Even Try

Okay, so you're probably thinking, "I can't even draw a stick figure, Kalesh. How is this going to help?" Doesn't matter. Seriously. Forget everything you think you know about art. This isn't about talent. This is about process. Here are some simple, no-pressure techniques to get you started, even if you feel completely overwhelmed:

  • Scribble Drawing: Grab a pen or crayon and just start scribbling on a piece of paper. Don't think. Don't try to make anything. Just let your hand move. Fast, slow, hard, light. Let the movement reflect what you're feeling inside. If you're agitated, let your scribbles be agitated. If you're feeling a dull ache, let them be dull. This is purely about releasing kinetic energy and emotion.
  • Blind Contour Drawing: Look at an object - anything, your hand, a cup, a plant - and place your pen on the paper. Now, without lifting your pen or looking at the paper, slowly trace the outline of the object with your eyes. Your hand moves as your eyes move. It forces you to really see, and it takes your mind off trying to "draw well." It's meditative and grounding.
  • Color Your Feelings: Get some crayons, pastels, or paints. Close your eyes for a moment and just tune into whatever sensation or emotion is strongest right now. What color does that feel like? What shape? What texture? Then open your eyes and put that color and shape onto the paper. Don't overthink it. Just let the color flow. If fear feels like jagged black lines, draw jagged black lines. If a strange energy feels like swirling purple, do that.
  • Expressive Journaling with Art: You know The Artist's Way Morning Pages Journal by Julia Cameron (paid link)? It's about getting thoughts out. Do that, but add drawings. Write for a bit, then draw a bit. No rules. Just let whatever comes out, come out. Sometimes the words will lead to a drawing, sometimes the drawing will spark a thought. It's all connected.
  • Mandala Drawing: A simple circle on a piece of paper can be incredibly centering. Just start drawing patterns, shapes, or colors within that circle, working your way from the center out or the outside in. There's something ancient and calming about mandalas. They represent wholeness, and that's something we often lose sight of during a kundalini experience.
  • Collage: If drawing feels too direct, try cutting out images, words, and textures from magazines and gluing them onto a piece of paper. You're creating a visual story of your inner world without having to "make" anything from scratch. It's about selecting and arranging, which can be less intimidating.

You don't need expensive supplies. A cheap notebook, a pencil, some crayons from the dollar store - that's enough. The point isn't the finished product; it's the act of creation, the release, the communication. It's about giving form to the formless, giving a voice to the unspeakable. Does that land? I hope so.

What Happens When You Create

When you engage in this kind of expressive art, a few things happen:

  • You Externalize the Internal: That terrifying, overwhelming feeling that's inside you? You put it outside of you, onto the paper. It's no longer just in your head. You can look at it. You can interact with it. It becomes less threatening because it's no longer an amorphous monster trapped within.
  • You Process Emotion: Art is a non-verbal way to process intense emotions - fear, anger, grief, confusion, even strange ecstasies. It allows you to feel them fully, move through them, and sometimes even transform them.
  • You Gain Insight: Sometimes, after you've created something, you'll look at it and suddenly understand something about what you're going through. A symbol will emerge, a pattern will become clear, and you'll have a moment of "aha!" It's your unconscious mind communicating with your conscious mind.
  • You Ground Yourself: The physical act of making art - the feel of the crayon on paper, the smell of paint, the focus required - can be incredibly grounding. It brings you back into your body, into the present moment, away from the swirling anxieties of your mind.
  • You Reclaim Agency: When kundalini hits, it can feel like you've lost all control. Your body, your mind, your life - it all feels hijacked. Creating something, anything, even just a scribble, is an act of agency. You are making a choice. You are creating something from nothing. It reminds you that you still have some power, some control, even in the midst of chaos.

I've seen it time and again, both in myself and others who've walked this path. The relief that comes from just getting it out. The validation of seeing your internal world manifest externally. It's a critical tool in navigating these intense states.

Integrating Art with Your Journey

This isn't a one-and-done thing. This is a practice. Make it a regular part of your routine, even if it's just 10-15 minutes a day. Keep a dedicated journal or sketchbook. Don't judge your work. Don't show it to anyone if you don't want to. This is for you, and only you.

You might find that as you continue, your "art" starts to change. Maybe it becomes less chaotic, more structured. Maybe new symbols or themes start to appear. Pay attention to those. They're messages from your deeper self, guiding you through this process.

If you're looking for more insight into what you're experiencing, books like The Kundalini Experience by Lee Sannella (paid link) or The Kundalini Guide by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link) can provide some context, but remember, your experience is unique. Art helps you map *your* unique experience.

And speaking of grounding, sometimes the physical sensations are just too much. Have you ever tried a Vibration Therapy Plate - Whole Body Vibration (paid link)? For some, the gentle vibration can help move stuck energy or just offer a different kind of sensory input that can be really helpful when you're feeling overwhelmed. It's another way to engage with your body and its sensations in a controlled way, which can be really important when kundalini is active.

So yeah, you're scared. You're alone. But you've got tools. You've got a way to communicate what's happening inside. Pick up that pencil. Make a mark. Let it out. You're stronger than you think, and you'll get through this. One weird, beautiful, terrifying drawing at a time.

This is not a substitute for professional mental health care. If you're in crisis, please reach out to a qualified therapist or call 988.
author: Kalesh
